It felt, and still feels, that there's a real need for a new big idea in America—a new animating idea about America's place in the world and how we'll compete in a totally different geopolitical situation than after World War II. America hasn't had a real competitor in a long time, so this is new. The Reagan ideas around government responded to FDR's ideas, which were losing utility because they slowed growth. Reagan unlocked growth with smaller government. Now, the geopolitical environment has changed, America has changed, and our country is hungry for a big idea about our future. That's been a big area of interest for me. I love asking people in different domains, 'What does education in America look like a hundred years from now?' We invented our education system a hundred years ago. It's pretty new. So the question is what can it look like a hundred years from now? That's interesting because we don't have long-run centralized planning in the US. In a world where we might need more of it, despite diversity serving America well, I think there's a hunger for direction. I'm not sure where that leadership is coming from today.
turns.zh: 这些大思想在提出后的几十年里往往会持续影响美国政治。它们通常不是某个人的想法，而是通过研究、知识工作、公众情绪以及我们试图解决的问题而涌现的。在那一刻，我感觉美国正接近从里根思想中获得实际效用的尾声。当时和现在我都觉得，美国迫切需要一个新的宏大思想——一个关于美国在世界上的地位以及我们如何在一个与二战后完全不同的地缘政治局势中竞争的新思想。美国很久没有真正的竞争对手了，所以这对美国来说是新的。里根关于政府的理念是对罗斯福思想的回应，后者因拖慢增长而失去效用。里根通过更小规模的政府释放了增长。现在，地缘政治环境变了，美国变了，我们的国家渴望一个关于未来的宏大思想。这一直是我非常感兴趣的领域。我喜欢问不同领域的人：“一百年后美国的教育会是什么样子？”我们的教育体系是一百年前发明的，还很新。所以问题是一百年后它能变成什么样？这很有趣，尤其是因为美国没有长期的中央规划。在一个我们可能更需要它的世界里，尽管多样性很好地服务了美国，我认为人们渴望方向。我不确定今天的领导力来自哪里。

turns.en: Yeah, I think it's still emerging. People are experimenting with different ideas, but that signals we're nearing the end of the Reagan era. People are testing new ideas to see what works. You see folks talking about the abundance movement, or the world splitting into regional spheres—like the Americas as a regional sphere for the US, which we may not have a choice about. It's too expensive to defend the whole world's oceans. But what does that mean? Global trade was enabled by US supremacy and our ability to defend shipping channels. I think there's a sense that we've overextended ourselves in protecting ourselves and allies. People are putting forward new ideas, but I don't think we've seen a new idea for the future break through yet.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为它还在涌现。人们在尝试不同的想法，但这表明我们正接近里根时代的尾声。人们在测试新想法，看看哪些有效。你看到有人在谈论丰裕运动，或者世界分裂成区域势力范围——比如美洲成为美国的区域势力范围，我们可能别无选择。保卫全球海洋太昂贵了。但这意味着什么？全球贸易是由美国的霸权和我们保卫全球航运通道的能力支撑的。我觉得有一种感觉是我们过度扩张了，在保护自己和盟友方面。人们正在提出新想法，但我认为还没有一个关于未来的新想法真正突破。

turns.en: Yeah, I agree. This thinking about the long term has inspired you. You're the head of a global organization. Do you apply that to Snap? Do you think about the long-term vision for this organization?
turns.zh: 是的，我同意。这种对长期的思考启发了你。你是一家全球组织的负责人。你会把这种思考应用到 Snap 上吗？你会考虑这个组织的长期愿景吗？

turns.en: 100%. That's exactly why 10 years ago, when we were a very small company with barely the resources to invest, we said we're going to reinvent the computer and make it more human by creating glasses. That has been our driving mission for the last 10 years. It's required a lot of sacrifice. We've invested a ton. We could have optimized for profitability and growth, which Wall Street loves, but instead we've consistently and deliberately invested in reinventing the computer because that's the impact we think we can have on the world. One thing that's unique about Snap, especially given our voting structure—Bobby and I control the company—we're able to invest over the long term. We always think of that as our chief strategic advantage. Many public companies can't invest long-term because shareholders pressure near-term profits. So we can consistently and deliberately invest in this long-term vision, which is a huge advantage.
turns.zh: 百分之百。这正是为什么十年前，当我们还是一家非常小的公司、几乎没有资源可以投资时，我们就说我们要重新发明计算机，通过制造眼镜让它更人性化。这过去十年一直是我们的使命。这需要很多牺牲。我们投入了巨资。我们本可以优化盈利和增长，华尔街喜欢这些，但我们却持续而刻意地投资于重新发明计算机，因为我们认为这是我们可以对世界产生的影响。Snap 的一个独特之处在于，由于我们的投票结构——鲍比和我控制公司——我们能够进行长期投资。我们一直把这视为我们的主要战略优势。许多上市公司无法进行长期投资，因为股东施压要求短期利润。所以我们能够持续而刻意地投资于这个长期愿景，这是一个巨大的优势。

turns.en: You've kept that as a strategic advantage. Is this an asset you protect very carefully, because maintaining that is hard in companies. So how has it changed you? You started this very young, and now you're a little more seasoned. How has your wisdom grown in the last 14 years?
turns.zh: 你一直把这视为战略优势。这是一个你非常小心保护的资产吗？因为在公司里维持这一点很难。那么它如何改变了你？你非常年轻时就创办了公司，现在你更有经验了。在过去的 14 年里，你的智慧是如何增长的？

turns.en: Oh my goodness. Building a company is virtually all about people. So the biggest journey I've been on is going from drawing the screens of the app and the little ghost icon to leading people—that's now my core responsibility. People leadership is hard because people are different. It's so important as a leader to unlock the different and unique capabilities of all the people we work with. Leadership is not one size fits all. You have to adapt your style to bring out the best in different people and find ways to stay close to them, to understand the challenges they're going through.
turns.zh: 天哪。创办公司几乎完全关乎人。所以我最大的旅程是从绘制应用界面和小幽灵图标到领导人们——这现在是我的核心责任。领导力很难，因为人是不同的。作为领导者，释放我们共事的所有人不同且独特的能力非常重要。领导力不是一刀切的。你必须调整自己的风格，以激发出不同人的最佳状态，并找到与他们保持亲近、理解他们面临挑战的方法。

turns.en: I mean, I've just found that I've grown a lot, but in growing and changing, the one thing I found always works is just going and talking to people. So, if I want to understand what's going on, I want to connect with our team, I just go walk around our office, talk to people about what's going on, what they're experiencing. They're on the front lines. They're serving our customers. They're serving our community and they know instantly and better than I do what we can be doing better and differently. So, I think for me leadership is about just staying close to people and loving your people. You can't be a leader if you don't love your people. That's the prerequisite to leadership. I just feel so blessed to work with such great people. That to me has been the most exciting, most challenging, most humbling part of the journey, really learning how to be a leader. People are hard. I always say, with people fast is slow and slow is fast. You push them too hard, you're going to get resistance, but you go slowly, you'll get there faster.
turns.zh: 我发现自己成长了很多，但在成长和变化中，我发现唯一始终有效的方法就是去和人们交谈。所以，如果我想了解情况，想和团队建立联系，我就会在办公室走走，和人们聊聊正在发生的事、他们的经历。他们身处一线，服务客户，服务社区，他们能立刻——而且比我更清楚——知道我们在哪些方面可以做得更好、更不同。所以，我认为领导力就是贴近人们、关爱你的团队。我常对团队说，如果你不爱你的团队，你就成不了领导者。这是领导力的前提。我感到非常幸运能与如此优秀的人共事。对我来说，学习如何成为领导者，是整个旅程中最激动人心、最具挑战性、也最令人谦卑的部分。人很难搞。我常说，与人相处，快就是慢，慢就是快。你逼得太紧，就会遇到阻力；但慢慢来，反而能更快到达。

turns.en: Last question. At some point, everybody thought the world was flat. Some people still do. For the most part, we know that it's not. What are we getting wrong today? What 200 years from now we'll look back and be like, 'Wow, we really were off on that one.'
turns.zh: 最后一个问题。曾经，所有人都认为地球是平的。现在有些人仍然这么认为。但大体上，我们知道它不是。我们今天在什么事情上搞错了？200 年后，人们回顾时会说：‘哇，我们当时真的完全搞错了。’

turns.en: I think over a much longer extended period of time, we will find much more scientific evidence of the interconnectedness of all of us, in so many different ways. The interconnectedness of life on this planet. I think one of the things I love about America is how much we celebrate the individual, the ability to have unique and different ideas. We love a cowboy in America. I think that's such a strength in terms of who we are as a country. But at the same time, I think it's so clear in the limited life experience that I have that we're also so deeply interconnected in ways that maybe we can't explain today with the tools or the science or the methods we have available, but that will become abundantly clear in the future. Both tangible and intangible.
turns.zh: 我认为，在更长的时间尺度上，我们会找到更多科学证据，证明我们所有人以各种方式相互关联。这个星球上生命的相互关联。我喜欢美国的一点是，我们如此推崇个体，推崇拥有独特想法的能力。在美国，我们热爱牛仔。我认为这是我们作为国家的优势。但同时，在我有限的人生经历中，我也清楚地看到，我们同样深深地相互关联，这种关联方式可能无法用我们今天拥有的工具、科学或方法来解释，但在未来会变得非常清晰。既有有形的，也有无形的。

turns.en: All right. Well, thank you, Evan Spiegel. Thank you. I appreciate today.
turns.zh: 好的。那么，谢谢你，Evan Spiegel。谢谢。我很感激今天的对话。

turns.en: Yeah, it was great. Good fun.
turns.zh: 是的，非常棒。很有趣。

consensus.en: Ephemeral messaging reduces social pressure and enables authentic communication.
consensus.zh: 短暂消息减少社交压力，实现真实沟通。

consensus.en: Augmented reality integrates computing into the real world, enhancing shared experiences.
consensus.zh: 增强现实将计算融入现实世界，增强共享体验。

consensus.en: AI accelerates prototyping and shifts value from execution to creativity.
consensus.zh: AI加速原型开发，价值从执行转向创意。

consensus.en: Authentic content is more valued by users than AI-generated content.
consensus.zh: 用户更看重真实内容而非AI生成内容。

consensus.en: Long-term investment in vision is a strategic advantage for Snap.
consensus.zh: 长期投资愿景是Snap的战略优势。

consensus.en: America needs a new big idea to address current geopolitical and domestic challenges.
consensus.zh: 美国需要新的大理念来应对当前的地缘政治和国内挑战。

contrarian.en: Privacy and data minimization were initially criticized but are now crucial in AI age.
contrarian.zh: 隐私和数据最小化最初受批评，但在AI时代至关重要。

contrarian.en: Social media's chronological order for stories is more natural than reverse chronological.
contrarian.zh: 社交媒体的故事按时间顺序排列比倒序更自然。

contrarian.en: AI can be more trusted than humans as moderators in deliberative democracy.
contrarian.zh: 在协商民主中，AI作为主持人可能比人类更受信任。

contrarian.en: The US over-indexes investments in older people, contributing to youth nihilism.
contrarian.zh: 美国过度投资老年人，导致青年虚无主义。

contrarian.en: California's housing crisis stems from difficulty building, not just tech company impact.
contrarian.zh: 加州住房危机源于建设困难，而非仅科技公司影响。

contrarian.en: China's long-term planning contrasts with US reliance on periodic big ideas.
contrarian.zh: 中国的长期规划与美国依赖周期性大理念形成对比。
